title: Understanding and Sharing Personal Stories
explanation_chunks: Writers of autobiographies often finish a chapter or section with a 'reflective comment'. This is where they think back on an event and explain why it was important to them or what they learned from it.
When you answer questions about a text, it's really helpful to find 'short quotations'. These are short snippets of the author's own words that prove your answer.

Sometimes you need to change 'direct speech' (exact words spoken) into 'reported speech' (telling someone *what* was said). When you do this, you might need to change verbs to a different past tense and pronouns (like 'I' to 'she').

Good writers use 'precise verbs' and 'adverbial phrases' to make their descriptions super clear and exciting. Precise verbs tell you exactly how someone did something, and adverbial phrases tell you when or where.
examples: Finding a quotation: If asked how Jane was amazed, you might quote, 'I could hardly believe it!'
Changing direct to reported speech: If Jane said, 'I began to fear the chimp would not come,' in reported speech it becomes, 'Jane said that she had begun to fear the chimp would not come.'
Precise verbs and adverbial phrases: Instead of 'She walked quickly', you could say 'She sprinted urgently' or 'She dashed away immediately'.
key_points: Reflective comments show the author's thoughts and learning from events.
Use short quotations to support your answers about a text.
Transforming direct speech to reported speech often requires changing verbs and pronouns.
Use precise verbs and adverbial phrases to make your writing clear and impactful.

vocabulary.definition: Bushes and long grasses in a forest.
Enjoyed eating a large amount of food.
A thought or opinion shared by the writer, looking back at an event.
The exact words someone says, usually in quotation marks.
Telling someone what was said, without using the exact words.
Verbs like 'can', 'could', 'will', 'would', 'may', 'might', 'must', 'shall', 'should' that express possibility or necessity.
Words that take the place of nouns, like 'I', 'you', 'he', 'she', 'it', 'we', 'they'.
Verbs that describe an action very exactly (e.g., 'sprinted' instead of 'ran').

explain_again: This page helps us dig deeper into stories. We learn that authors sometimes tell us how they felt about an event (like a mini-lesson!). We also practice taking someone's exact words and changing them into our own words to tell the story. And remember, choosing the perfect word makes your writing shine!
fun_analogy: Changing direct speech to reported speech is like playing a game of 'Chinese Whispers' with words. The message stays mostly the same, but the way it's said changes a little bit along the way!
